source_text:
Voyager 1 launched from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977, sixteen days after its sister probe Voyager 2 — a counterintuitive ordering explained by the different trajectories NASA had chosen for the two spacecraft. Riding a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, the probe began a primary mission of five years: a planetary flyby of Jupiter and Saturn during a rare alignment of the outer planets that occurs roughly once every 176 years.

It reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979, sending back the first detailed images of the planet's faint ring system and discovering active volcanoes on its moon Io — the first observation of active volcanism beyond Earth. Twenty months later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 made its closest approach to Saturn, photographing the ring structure in unprecedented detail and capturing close-ups of the moon Titan. The Titan trajectory pushed Voyager 1 above the plane of the solar system, ending its planetary tour but setting it on a path toward interstellar space.

On February 14, 1990, at the urging of astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 turned its camera back toward home and photographed Earth from roughly 6 billion kilometers away. The image — a single pixel against the sun's scattered light — became known as the Pale Blue Dot. It was the spacecraft's final photograph; the cameras were powered down afterwards to conserve energy.

Voyager 1 crossed the heliopause on August 25, 2012, becoming the first human-made object to enter interstellar space. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated by a team led by Sagan, containing greetings in 55 languages, music, and images intended for any intelligence that might one day find it. Power comes from three radioisotope thermoelectric generators fueled by plutonium-238, whose 87.7-year half-life has slowly drained the spacecraft's energy budget.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is roughly 25 billion kilometers from Earth, traveling at about 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will become the first probe to cross one light-day of distance.

rounds.output_text:
Voyager 1 embarked on its journey from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. This launch occurred sixteen days after its counterpart, Voyager 2, a sequence that seemed reversed due to the distinct flight paths NASA assigned to the two probes. Utilizing a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket, the primary objective of the mission was five years of observation, involving flybys of Saturn and Jupiter during a fortunate, infrequent configuration of the outer planets, an alignment occurring approximately every 176 years.

The probe arrived at Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this encounter, it transmitted the initial detailed visuals of the planet’s faint ring system and discovered active volcanoes on its moon Io—marking the first documented instance of such volcanism beyond Earth. Twenty months following this Jupiter pass, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 reached its closest point to Saturn. It documented the ring system with unmatched clarity and captured detailed images of Titan. This Saturn trajectory propelled Voyager 1 beyond the solar system's plane, concluding its planetary circuit but directing it into interstellar space.

On February 14, 1990, following the recommendation of astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 aimed its instruments back toward Earth, taking a picture when it was approximately 6 billion kilometers distant. This solitary image, appearing as one pixel against the sunlight, earned the title Pale Blue Dot. This represented the last photograph taken by the spacecraft, after which the cameras were powered down to maintain energy reserves.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, crossing the heliopause boundary, making it the first human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated under Sagan’s leadership. This record contains greetings in 55 languages, alongside music and imagery intended for any civilization that might eventually locate it. Its power source relies on three radioisotope thermoelectric generators, fueled by plutonium-238, whose half-life of 87.7 years has steadily diminished the available energy.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated approximately 25 billion kilometers from Earth and is traveling at roughly 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will become the first probe to span a distance equivalent to one light-day.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is positioned about 25 billion kilometers from Earth while maintaining a speed nearing 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will attain the significant milestone of having traveled a distance equal to one light-day.
rounds.output_text:
Voyager 1 began its mission from Cape Canaveral on September 5, 1977. Although both probes utilized a Titan IIIE-Centaur rocket for launch, Voyager 1’s deployment occurred sixteen days subsequent to that of Voyager 2, despite the specific flight paths assigned by NASA. The primary goal of this endeavor was to conduct observations for a period of five years, during which both Jupiter and Saturn would be scrutinized in depth. This alignment of the distant planets, occurring roughly every 176 years, was a rare and welcome celestial event.

Voyager 1 reached Jupiter on March 5, 1979. During this transit, the probe relayed the first high-resolution images of Jupiter's faint rings and documented Io's active volcanism, thus establishing the initial record of volcanism outside of Earth. Two years later, on November 12, 1980, Voyager 1 performed its closest pass to Saturn. It collected highly detailed data concerning Saturn's ring system and successfully photographed Titan. This journey past Saturn propelled Voyager 1 beyond the solar system's confines, concluding its planetary tour and setting a course into interstellar space.

Following a suggestion from astronomer Carl Sagan, Voyager 1 aimed its instruments back toward Earth on February 14, 1990, transmitting one final photograph while it was approximately 6 billion kilometers distant. This solitary image, appearing as a single pixel against the sunlight, became famously known as the Pale Blue Dot. This transmission marked the last photograph transmitted by the spacecraft before the cameras were deactivated to conserve power.

Voyager 1 officially entered interstellar space on August 25, 2012, upon crossing the heliopause boundary, making it the inaugural human-made object to achieve this status. It carries the Golden Record, a 12-inch, gold-plated copper phonograph disc curated based on Sagan’s advice. This record contains greetings in 55 languages, alongside audiovisual material intended for any civilization that might eventually find it. Its power supply is derived from three radioisotope thermoelectric generators fueled by plutonium-238; the steady decay of this isotope, which possesses a half-life of 87.7 years, provides the requisite energy.

As of early 2026, Voyager 1 is situated about 25 billion kilometers from Earth while maintaining a velocity approaching 61,000 kilometers per hour. In November 2026, it will reach the important benchmark of having traveled a distance equivalent to one light-day.
